modes_checked.infrastructure: yellow — FUSE quick-check 2/5 STALE (data/wins.json, docs/index.html) + extended scan CLAUDE.md STALE. sections.json has caught up since 09:50Z (was stale then, fresh now). The NEW finding here: the prior report's hypothesis that 'build.js publish's workspace-sync is inconsistently covering sections.json vs wins.json' is REFUTED by today's evidence — the actual mechanism is that the decider bypasses `build.js publish` entirely (decider.md has no call to it; only `git push origin main` after `node build.js html`). The workspace gets no git→mount copy after a decider push. Only the scheduled `dome-workspace-sync` agent (every 4h) does any sync, and it only goes workspace→git, never git→workspace. So the workspace ONLY catches up on git-owned files when (a) an operator manually runs `build.js publish` or (b) some future reverse-sync fires. This is a systemic architectural gap, not a one-off bug. Classifying as 'moderate' rather than 'major' because the consumers of stale mount files are all protected: integrity clones fresh, social cross-checks GitHub raw, curmudgeon/decider clone fresh, analyst cross-checks counts. The stale mount only hurts when a human reads it manually or when a future agent is added without freshness protection. Disk 83% (1714MB free), unchanged.

infrastructure_findings.description: FUSE git→workspace coverage gap is ARCHITECTURAL, not a timing glitch. The decider never calls `build.js publish` — its patch workflow ends at `node build.js html` + `git add` + `git commit` + `git push origin main`. The `build.js publish` workspace-sync block only runs when an operator manually publishes or when a non-existent automated caller invokes it. The workspace-sync scheduled agent (every 4h) only copies workspace→clone, never git→workspace. So git-owned files (wins.json, sections.json, docs/index.html, CLAUDE.md, etc.) land on origin/main and the FUSE mount only catches up if somebody manually runs `build.js publish`. Today's observed pattern: sections.json was synced at some point (fresh now, was stale 80 min ago) but wins.json and docs/index.html remain stale vs main HEAD, with CLAUDE.md still at the 08:35Z operator snapshot rather than the 09:36Z version. The prior report's 'one-time sync glitch' hypothesis is refuted by consistent recurrence.
infrastructure_findings.evidence: `grep -n 'build.js publish' monitor/prompts/decider.md monitor/prompts/reference/decider-*.md` returns nothing — decider NEVER invokes publish. `grep -n 'git push' monitor/prompts/reference/decider-patches-and-selfapply.md` → line 178 `git push origin main` bare push with no subsequent workspace sync. workspace-sync.md Step 2 starts with 'Copy files from workspace to clone' (direction: mount→git only).
infrastructure_findings.why_not_self_fixed: This needs a PROP — candidate options are (a) have workspace-sync also do a git→workspace copy for git-owned files at the top of its run, (b) have decider call `build.js publish` instead of raw `git push`, (c) a dedicated reverse-sync agent. All three require careful thinking about race conditions and ownership. Not writing a PROP today because DIRECTIVE-002 already consumed the PROP budget; flagging for next Mode 2 run as PROP-010 candidate.
infrastructure_findings.recommended_next_action: tinker next Mode 2 run — write PROP-010 for git→workspace reverse sync in the workspace-sync agent. Low risk because it's adding a fetch step at the top of a read-only-from-git clone, and the 'git-owned' classification is already canonical (build.js OWNERSHIP and workspace-sync.md OWNED_BY_GIT) so the copy list is known.

findings.description: Decider Step E2 pop filter and curmudgeon Step 0b skip filter both use substring-only match against the reviews/ directory. Neither checks queue_id or reviewed_at-vs-pushed_at. Result: any target_id with pre-existing old reviews gets auto-popped on re-push, and curmudgeon skips it thinking it's already reviewed. 10 confirmed unreviewed pops in the 72h window (qids 165-174). SEC-4.1, WIN-058, WIN-062, WIN-058b all went live post-EXP-181/EXP-182 integration with no adversarial check.
findings.evidence: monitor/prompts/decider.md lines 194-205 (hasReview=reviews.some(f=>searchTerms.some(t=>f.includes(t)))); monitor/prompts/curmudgeon.md Step 0b procedure line 2 (identical substring match); priority-queue.json history entries qids 165-174 with popped_by=decider and no matching review file; git show 29e11ac and e4630ec confirm push+pop in same decider run.
findings.suggested_fix: See PROP-009.json — replace both substring matches with strict queue_id match + soft reviewed_at>pushed_at fallback + operator bypass.
findings.why_not_self_fixed: Requires coordinated prompt changes on decider.md AND curmudgeon.md, operator approval of the precondition wording, and a 3-day shadow migration. Not a mechanical fix. Formalized as PROP-009.

findings.description: Orthogonal to PROP-009: SEC-4.1 review files show cycle numbering going backwards — c3.json at 2026-04-18T07:42Z (queue_id 158, popped yesterday 23:26Z) was written AFTER c4.json at 2026-04-18T05:12Z (queue_id 163, popped 05:32Z today). Likely cause: concurrent curmudgeon runs on differently-aged clones determining cycle number via 'highest existing cycle + 1' — one run's clone missed the other's c4, computed next=3, and wrote c3 hours late. Does not corrupt review content but misleads chronology readers.
findings.evidence: curl raw SEC-4.1.c3.json reviewed_at=2026-04-18T07:42:00Z cycle=3 queue_id=158; SEC-4.1.c4.json reviewed_at=2026-04-18T05:12:46.475Z cycle=4 queue_id=163
findings.suggested_fix: Separate PROP required. Options: (1) curmudgeon uses reviewed_at ordering instead of cycle number for chronology; (2) atomic cycle allocation via a counter file; (3) cycle number is purely cosmetic and gets recomputed by a post-hoc script from reviewed_at ordering. Recommend (1) — cycle-numbering is display sugar, not semantic. But this is speculation; operator should decide.
findings.why_not_self_fixed: Orthogonal to this run's directive (DIRECTIVE-002 explicitly asks to flag for separate follow-up). Did not write PROP-010 for it because tinker's one-mode-per-run rule caps work to PROP-009 today.

recommendations.description: Review and approve PROP-009 wording. Decide: 3-day shadow migration (recommended) vs immediate enforcement. Land decider.md + curmudgeon.md patches in a single commit.

Decide whether to open a separate PROP for the SEC-4.1 cycle-numbering-backwards anomaly or defer. Recommendation: defer unless pattern repeats in next 7 days. If deferred, tinker will auto-reopen if it sees another cycle-backwards case.

Next Mode 2 run: write PROP-010 to add git→workspace reverse sync in the workspace-sync agent. The FUSE staleness pattern is now confirmed architectural — 3 consecutive days of stale git-owned files on the mount. Fix: workspace-sync clones fresh at start (already does), then BEFORE copying workspace→git, copies git-owned files git→workspace. Run-safe because git-owned files are single-writer from the clone direction.

PROP-005 (decider pre-flight work check) — still proposed 13th consecutive run. Recommend merge-or-close this week. Pipeline is in BAU, not quiet, so its marginal value is low right now; closing it is reasonable if we decide the cost of keeping an open proposal we won't use.

the_other_side.prop_009_strongest_objection: The bug is real but its total impact over 72h is bounded — 10 unreviewed pops, of which the content was all already curmudgeon-reviewed in PRIOR cycles. So the 'adversarial review gap' is not a review gap in absolute terms; it's a post-integration-retest gap. Someone could reasonably argue that if EXP-181 and EXP-182 were themselves adversarially reviewed (pre-integration expansion-draft reviews, which they were not in full — see directive concerns_to_address item 3), the post-integration review is redundant for the fraction of changes that didn't introduce new material. Against this: post-integration reviews catch integration-time bugs — typos in the sections.json merge, broken anchors, truncated citations, kappa-badge math errors — that the pre-integration review cannot see because the prose doesn't exist yet. The 72h evidence does NOT show whether any of the 10 unreviewed pops would have produced critical findings; a shadow period is specifically designed to answer that empirical question rather than assume.
the_other_side.migration_objection: 3-day shadow is conservative; an alternative is 'enforce immediately and fix forward on any queue backlog'. If PROP-009 strict-match is correct, curmudgeon's first run should write a queue_id-tagged review within 4h, and the decider's next run pops it. Worst case if curmudgeon fails to tag queue_id: the soft fallback (reviewed_at > pushed_at) catches it. So a 0-day shadow is arguably safe. The counter is: we've been wrong about 'safe' before (PROP-003 took 8 runs to close, operators landed changes mid-migration, and a shadow lets us catch curmudgeon clones that are stale and write reviews with misleading timestamps). 3 days is cheap insurance.
the_other_side.orthogonal_cycle_numbering_objection: It could be argued the SEC-4.1.c3/c4 backwards-cycle case is BENIGN — the decider's pop gate doesn't care about cycle numbers, only queue_id and reviewed_at. So why fix it? Counter: cycle numbers are the primary way humans navigate review history in the reviews/ directory. Backwards cycles mislead manual triage and can cause an operator to apply a stale review's patch over a fresher review's superseding patch. Cost of a fix is likely small (cycle = rank of reviewed_at among same-target reviews, recomputed on read) but the operator should decide the priority.
the_other_side.fuse_staleness_objection: Adding a git→workspace reverse sync to workspace-sync sounds simple but may race with curmudgeon/analyst runs that are actively writing to the FUSE mount. Example: workspace-sync does git pull at T0, copies wins.json git→mount at T1. Meanwhile analyst at T0.5 was reading the old wins.json from the mount to produce an expansion. Race outcome: analyst's expansion is based on pre-T1 data. This is actually no worse than today's world (analyst reads whatever is on the mount at read time), but it introduces a new flavor of race. Mitigation: put the reverse-sync AFTER the workspace→git push so mount→git sync happens first on the old content, then git→mount sync updates the mount for the next run. This is how the PROP-010 proposal should be structured when it's written. Not a blocker, but worth flagging for the PROP-010 writer.

notes_to_future_tinker: DIRECTIVE-002 deliverables fully addressed in PROP-009 sections — check those before re-writing if a followup is needed.

If PROP-009 lands in shadow mode, you will see entries appear in monitor/integrity/prop-009-shadow.jsonl. Track the rate and composition daily. When the ratio of 'legitimate block' to 'false block' stabilizes and we've seen at least one strict_queue_id pop in history, recommend enforcing.
The SEC-4.1 cycle-backwards orthogonal finding: do NOT open a separate PROP unless you see another cycle-backwards case. One-off is likely a race; two is a pattern worth fixing.
PROP-005 has been 'proposed' for 13 runs. If pipeline ever re-enters quiet mode (8h cadences, zero curmudgeon queue, analyst only working human-notes), revisit. Until then, consider closing.

FUSE staleness architectural gap → PROP-010 is the obvious next candidate but save it for a proper Mode 2 run when you have time to think through the race conditions (see 'the_other_side.fuse_staleness_objection' for the nuance).

If the next operator directive arrives within 2 hours of this run, it is reasonable to do a THIRD run and load the dispatcher + proposals module only (Mode 4 has lower context cost than Mode 3). The 09:50Z → 10:13Z → possibly-next cadence is driven by operator work rate, which is not a tinker-configurable thing.

workspace-sync agent runs at 11:05Z — it will NOT push this tinker report or PROP-009 back to git because both files are in 'append_only' / 'append_only_glob' directories (monitor/tinker/ and monitor/tinker/proposals/). Workspace-sync's append-only walker will copy them mount→clone, then commit. Expected commit message pattern: 'Workspace sync: 2026-04-18T11:05:xxZ'.

The PROP-009 find/replace blocks contain escaped characters (backslash-d, backslash-n). When the operator applies the patch via sed or node-apply-patches, verify the regex escapes survived JSON serialization — specifically the `\\d+` in secMatch and the `\\n` in appendFileSync. If they didn't, the patch will fail to apply; operator should hand-transcribe those two lines.
